description: Use this skill when an SLO is being set, revised, or validated for fitness — specifically when determining whether the SLO number is appropriate, defensible, and agreed upon by all parties who will be bound by its consequences.

test_prompts.prompt: Our head of sales just committed to enterprise customers that we'd have five nines of availability. That's the SLO now, apparently. The SRE team says we can't operationally defend 99.999% without burning out the on-call and the dev team lead says a feature freeze at that level would kill the roadmap. How do we handle this?
test_prompts.rationale: SLO set unilaterally by one party (sales/leadership) without the other parties' involvement or agreement. The skill's Scenario 1 is the direct match — the negotiation gate must run. Each party holds their appropriate veto (SRE: toil cap, dev: feature velocity, PM/product: user experience), and all three must reach tripartite approval before the SLO is valid.

test_prompts.prompt: We wrote an error budget policy, circulated it to all three stakeholders, and the SRE lead refused to sign. They say the proposed 99.95% target would require constant on-call escalation to hit, which would push toil above the 50% cap. The product manager says we need 99.95% for the customer experience. We're stuck. What do we do?
test_prompts.rationale: This is the negotiation gate working correctly — the SRE veto is a valid engineering signal, not a negotiating tactic. The skill prescribes iterating on the SLO target (not the policy), and offers specific options: lower the target, specify what automation investment would enable the tighter target, or adopt an aspirational SLO while the automation is built.

test_prompts.prompt: We had a major outage last week and the post-mortem is recommending we tighten our SLO from 99.9% to 99.95%. The SRE team is strongly in favor after what they just went through. Can we just update the SLO document and move on, or is there more to it?
test_prompts.rationale: Post-incident SLO tightening without running the tripartite gate is specifically named as a failure pattern. Any new SLO — even one generated by a post-incident review — must pass through the negotiation gate. High-emotion post-incident contexts are exactly when organizational pressure can produce unilateral SLO changes that haven't been validated for developer and product manager acceptability.

test_prompts.prompt: Our error budget policy has been ratified for six months and it's working well. Our budget just hit zero yesterday. The policy says we freeze releases, but the dev team is asking whether a minor config change counts as a 'release' under the policy definition. How do we interpret this?
test_prompts.rationale: The SLO and policy are ratified and in force. This is a policy interpretation and execution question, not an SLO fitness or negotiation question. The error-budget-policy-framework skill governs execution of a ratified policy, including definitional edge cases like whether a config change triggers a freeze.

test_prompts.prompt: Our SLO is ratified and green for the quarter. We measure availability at 99.95%. But user satisfaction scores are declining and we're seeing more support tickets. Is our SLO set correctly?
test_prompts.rationale: This is a calibration question — the SLO may be measuring the wrong thing (Met/Low satisfaction cell in the decision matrix). The slo-decision-matrix applies here, specifically the 'wrong SLI' diagnosis. The negotiation gate would only be triggered after the matrix identifies a calibration change (tighten or loosen) that requires tripartite approval.

test_prompts.prompt: We're debating whether to measure SLO compliance at the CDN edge or at the application server. The numbers are meaningfully different — 99.97% at the edge versus 99.91% at the app server. Which measurement point should we use as the SLI?
test_prompts.rationale: This is a technical SLI specification dispute about measurement point accuracy, explicitly excluded by the boundary section: 'The dispute is purely technical (e.g., which SLI measurement point is most accurate) rather than about the organizational fitness of the SLO.' Technical disputes go to the SLI specification layer. The negotiation gate is triggered by organizational fitness, not measurement methodology.

test_prompts.prompt: We're a DevOps shop — our engineers own development and operations. We don't have separate SRE and product management roles. We have a tech lead, a product owner, and the same engineers who write code and handle on-call. Can we still run the tripartite approval process, or does it require separate teams?
test_prompts.rationale: Resolved as invoke because the skill's author blind spots explicitly acknowledge this case: 'In organizations where engineers do their own operations (DevOps model), the three parties may be the same people wearing different hats. The gate still applies but requires explicit hat-switching.' The skill should guide them through how to run the gate with hats, not tell them it doesn't apply.

test_prompts.prompt: Our new microservice is launching in beta next week. We don't have real production traffic data yet. Someone suggested we set a placeholder SLO of 99.9% just to have something in place. Is that the right approach?
test_prompts.rationale: Resolved as skip because the boundary section explicitly excludes pre-GA services without sufficient production data: 'Use the engagement model phase structure instead (Chapter 18): SLOs should be defined before GA, not after.' A placeholder SLO ratified without production data cannot be operationally defended by SRE and cannot be honestly evaluated by the product manager — the gate cannot function correctly without baseline data.
